title: Workload and Recognition Trap
summary: AI adoption fails when efficiency gains increase workload without recognition, role redesign, or evaluation changes.
evidence_excerpt: Efficiency gains that merely increase workload without adjusting recognition or evaluation create resistance and burnout.
recommended_use: Consider when evaluating proposed workflow opportunities for their impact on staff workload and recognition.

title: Connect Improvement to Evaluation
summary: AI-enabled improvement should be connected to evaluation, recognition, or knowledge-sharing value.
evidence_excerpt: Staff are more likely to adopt AI when improved outcomes are reflected in evaluations or shared as knowledge contributions.
recommended_use: Reference when designing adoption risk notes or human-system notes that address incentives.

title: Train on Real Workflows, Not Tools
summary: AI training should focus on changing real workflows, not only teaching tool usage.
evidence_excerpt: Training that only teaches tool operation without addressing workflow changes has limited adoption impact.
recommended_use: Reference when workflow opportunities include training recommendations.

title: Structured Knowledge Sources Required
summary: Effective AI workflows require structured, searchable, reliable knowledge sources.
evidence_excerpt: Without organized knowledge sources, AI-assisted workflows produce inconsistent or unreliable outputs.
recommended_use: Apply when extracting knowledge sources from interaction notes or evaluating workflow feasibility.

title: Document Failure Cases for Human Review Boundaries
summary: Failure cases and exceptions should be documented because they define where human review is needed.
evidence_excerpt: Known failure modes and edge cases clarify where automated processes require human judgment and oversight.
recommended_use: Reference when extracting failure cases from interaction notes or defining human review boundaries.

title: Start with Small Repeated Workflow Wins
summary: AI adoption should usually start with small, repeated workflow-efficiency opportunities before broad automation or revenue-focused projects.
evidence_excerpt: Starting with small, repetitive, low-risk workflow improvements builds confidence and demonstrates value before scaling.
recommended_use: Consider when prioritizing workflow opportunities or recommending pilot candidates.

title: Experienced Staff Guide AI Design
summary: Experienced staff should guide AI workflow design because they can judge whether outputs are useful and correct.
evidence_excerpt: Staff with deep domain knowledge are best positioned to evaluate AI outputs and guide workflow integration.
recommended_use: Reference when workflow opportunities include staff roles or when designing human-system notes.

title: AI Captures Experienced Staff Knowledge
summary: AI should support experienced workers by helping turn their knowledge into repeatable workflows.
evidence_excerpt: AI tools can help encode expert knowledge into standardized, repeatable processes that less experienced staff can follow.
recommended_use: Reference when extracting workflow opportunities that involve knowledge capture or process documentation.

title: Scale Proven Patterns, Not Zero-to-Strategy
summary: AI is better for scaling proven patterns than creating strategy from zero.
evidence_excerpt: AI supplements rather than replaces human judgment for strategic or creative work that requires contextual understanding.
recommended_use: Consider when evaluating whether a proposed AI use case is appropriate for the current maturity level.

title: Decompose Workflows Before Choosing Tools
summary: Workflows should be decomposed into smaller steps before deciding whether each step belongs to generative AI, browser automation, OCR, database search, templates, or human judgment.
evidence_excerpt: Different workflow steps are best served by different tools — decomposition reveals which approach fits each step.
recommended_use: Reference when analyzing workflow steps or designing AI-supported processes.

title: Generative AI Is Not Always the Right Tool
summary: Generative AI is not always the right tool; some workflow steps may require non-AI automation or specialized tools.
evidence_excerpt: Over-applying generative AI to steps better served by simpler automation creates unnecessary complexity and risk.
recommended_use: Consider when evaluating proposed AI insertion points — not every workflow step needs generative AI.

title: Human Review for High-Stakes Work
summary: Human review should remain explicit for externally visible, quality-sensitive, private, or judgment-heavy work.
evidence_excerpt: Maintaining explicit human review for high-stakes decisions preserves quality, privacy, and accountability.
recommended_use: Reference when extracting failure cases, designing human-system notes, or defining human review requirements.

title: AI Talent Develops Through Workflow Learning
summary: Effective AI adoption should develop people who can identify inefficient workflows themselves, break those workflows into steps, test small AI-assisted improvements, and share the results as reusable organizational knowledge.
evidence_excerpt: AI training should focus on workflow impact by helping staff identify inefficient processes, test small improvements, review results, and share examples internally.
recommended_use: Reference when planning training, evaluating workflow opportunities, or turning reviewed automation examples into reusable organizational knowledge.
